# Physiology and Cognition Map

## Visual-channel bottleneck

Short-form video asks the viewer to process speech, text, face, action, background, UI, and motion at once. When audio is off, subtitles become the primary language channel and visual load increases.

## Foveal and peripheral vision

Detailed text requires foveal/parafoveal processing. Peripheral text can orient but should not carry dense sentences. COD therefore uses readable foveal microphrases and peripheral icons/markers only as cues.

## Visual crowding

Text recognition worsens in clutter. This explains why overlays near native captions, platform UI, stickers, or patterned backgrounds often fail. COD requires crowding-aware placement and contrast fallbacks.

## Visual working memory

Viewers cannot hold many visual/verbal chunks while watching motion. COD uses one idea per screen and structured beats such as Problem → Cause → Fix.

## Audiovisual speech

Visible mouth/face information can support speech understanding. COD protects the mouth during speech-heavy clips and protects eyes during emotional clips.

## Meaning-guided attention

Attention is guided not only by brightness, movement, and contrast, but by meaning. COD therefore prioritizes semantic overlays that reveal the mental model rather than merely making text “pop.”

## Reward and scroll behavior

Short-form video is a low-commitment, high-exit environment. Overlays must orient quickly, reduce uncertainty, and deliver payoff without relying on confusion-driven retention.
